---
title: "19. AI as Programmer"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb04_observational_descriptive_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** When you hand the writing of analysis code to an AI,
> you decide **what quantity that code must compute and over which set of cases**,
> then verify it computed *that* quantity before you believe the number. A cell
> that runs without an error is not a result you have earned.

## Why this decision matters

> *"Do not read me the number. Tell me what set it was computed over. If the code
> only saw the buildings that already had sensors, you have not described the
> campus. You have described the sensors."*
> — a **facilities-safety reviewer**, asked to act on an indoor-air-quality report

An AI can write your analysis code in seconds, and it will run. That is exactly
where the danger sits. The tool writes fluent code for the quantity it *thinks* you
asked for, over whatever cases sit in the dataframe you handed it. Paste the printed
number into your report and you have signed your name to a quantity you never
checked, over a set you never chose. The reviewer above does not care that the code
was clean. She cares what it counted, and over whom.

## The concept

The tool is the programmer. You are the researcher. Two decisions never move to the
programmer's side of the desk, and this chapter is about holding them.

- **Descriptive summary**: a number or picture that reports what is actually in the
  cases you can see, such as a share, an average, or a full distribution. Example:
  the fraction of monitored rooms whose air crosses a health line.
- **Quantity of interest**: the one exact number your code is supposed to produce,
  pinned down in words *before* you ask for any code. Example: "the share of
  academic buildings whose peak-hour CO₂ exceeds 1000 ppm."
- **Frame**: the concrete set of cases your data actually covers, which is often
  smaller than the group your question is about. Example: only the buildings that
  already have sensors bolted to a wall.
- **Convenience sample**: a set of cases collected because they were easy to reach,
  not drawn to stand in for a population. Example: those sensored buildings, which
  tend to be the newest and best-ventilated on campus.

Here is the trap the chapter closes. A tool can compute a flawless share over the
wrong frame, and the code still finishes with a green check. **A clean run is not a
correct result**: a cell can execute with no error and still compute a different
number than your question needs. So you own two things the programmer cannot. First,
you define the quantity of interest and the frame it runs over, in plain words, before
you delegate. Second, you verify the returned code computed *that* quantity over
*that* frame, not a plausible neighbor.

## A worked example

You want to describe indoor air quality across campus for the question *what share
of academic buildings exceed the healthy CO₂ threshold during class hours?* You have
a spreadsheet of sensor readings, and you ask an AI to write the Python that returns
the share above 1000 ppm.

It writes six clean lines. They run. The printout says **6% of buildings exceed the
threshold**, and the temptation is to write "campus air is fine." Instead you read
what the code did, and two things jump out. The dataframe holds only the eight
buildings that already carry permanent sensors, the newest and best ventilated on
campus. That is a **convenience sample** standing in silently for the whole campus.
The code also calls `.dropna()`, and the dropped rows are the readings where cheap
sensors failed, clustered in the oldest, most crowded rooms. The 6% describes the best
buildings with their worst rooms deleted.

Re-anchor to your **quantity of interest**: the share over *all* academic buildings on
the registrar's list, peak-hour readings kept. Recompute over the full list, flag the
failed-sensor rooms rather than dropping them, and the honest share is far higher. The
tool wrote correct code for the wrong question. You did the research by deciding what
the code was allowed to count.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb04 — Observational descriptive research**. Open it
in Colab with the badge above. There you hand cells to Gemini and make it earn your
trust: you ask it to explain, line by line, why ten random samples huddle around a
frame's true mean while one convenience channel misses it by nearly twelve years,
then confirm the range it quotes against the numbers your own cell actually printed.
That gap between the tool's fluent story and your printout is this chapter's lesson,
run live.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a
request for a verdict.

**Locate the standard idiom.**

```text
Act as a Python data assistant. I need the share of rows above a threshold,
computed only over a subset I will name. Before any code, name the standard pandas
idiom for a threshold share over a filtered subset and the exact argument that
controls whether missing values are dropped. Only name functions you are sure exist.
```
After running, verify: open the pandas docs and confirm the method and that argument
exist and default as described. Counters *confident fabrication* (an invented method
or argument arrives as confidently as a real one).

**Delegate, then demand the choices back as a table.**

```text
Write code that returns the share of academic buildings whose peak-hour CO2 exceeds
1000 ppm. Then return a table of every choice you made: which rows you kept, whether
you dropped any and why, the exact set the share is computed over, and whether you
reported a share or an average.
```
After running, verify: check the row count before and after the code, and confirm the
set it computed over is the one your question names. Counters *silent scope change*
(a share quietly computed over the sensored buildings, not the campus).

**Red-team the number.**

```text
Here is my claim: "6% of academic buildings exceed the CO2 threshold." Act as a
hostile facilities reviewer. Name every way this code could have counted the wrong
quantity or this share could mislead. Do not rewrite the claim for me.
```
After running, verify: if it only praises the number, push back and demand the single
worst flaw. Counters *sycophantic agreement* (praise that reviews your ego, not your
evidence).

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
You define **what quantity the code computes** and **which set of cases it runs
over**, and you decide **what the number is allowed to claim**. The tool can write
the filter, the aggregation, and the plot, but it cannot decide that the sensored
buildings speak for the campus, or that a share with the worst rooms dropped is
honest. You own the final sentence, its frame, and its boundary.
:::

## An AI failure case

You ask for the threshold share and the tool returns tidy code that prints, with full
confidence, "**6% of buildings exceed 1000 ppm — air quality is within norms.**" The
code has no error and reads beautifully. The trap is buried in two lines: the input
dataframe was already limited to buildings with installed sensors, and a `.dropna()`
silently removed the rooms where sensors failed. The share is real arithmetic over the
wrong set.

You catch it by reading the code, not its summary. Printing the dataframe's shape
shows eight buildings, not the campus. Printing the row count before and after
`.dropna()` shows how many readings vanished, and inspecting which reveals the
worst-ventilated rooms. Recomputing over the registrar's full building list, failures
flagged instead of dropped, collapses the reassuring 6%. A green check certified that
the code ran, never that it answered your question.

## Verification laboratory

Verify this chapter's key result, the threshold share, with **alternative code** from
the [verification guide](https://github.com/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/ai_resources/verification_guide.md):
recompute the same quantity a different way and compare. If the tool used a filtered
`.mean()` on a boolean mask, recount with a `groupby` over the same set and confirm the
two shares match to the digit. Then catch the silent trap: print the dataframe's shape
and the row count before and after any drop, so a disappearing row cannot hide inside a
clean number. Two roads to one share is a verified result. Two roads to two shares
means the code, not the campus, is what you measured.

## Project transfer

This is the code half of your **M4 observational descriptive design audit**. Before
you delegate the code that produces any descriptive number for your project, write
your quantity of interest and its frame in one sentence each, the way nb04 has you
name the target population, accessible population, frame, and sample. Then verify the
returned code computed that quantity over that frame, and record the row-count check
that proved it in your AI Research Ledger: the task, the tool, the decision, the named
check. That verified number is what your frame-and-sample stack rests on when you walk
a skeptic through it at Friday's studio.

## Defend your decision

In four sentences, defend one descriptive number you delegated code to compute. Name
the exact quantity of interest and the set of cases the code ran over. State the one
verification that confirmed the code computed *that* quantity, not a neighbor. Give the
boundary, what the number does not establish about the population beyond your frame,
and close with your remaining uncertainty and the next check you would run to close it.
